---
title: "Sample Results Tables for Six Indian Companies"
author: "OpenAI"
date: "2026-03-12"
output:
  word_document: default
  pdf_document: default
  html_document:
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
---

# Overview

This document provides **illustrative sample tables** for six Indian companies:

-   Coromandel
-   JSW Steel
-   BASF India
-   Ambuja Cement
-   Aarti Industries Limited
-   GNFC

These values are **sample placeholders for manuscript drafting only**. They are **not empirical results** until the full pipeline is run on the company reports.

# Table 1. Company-level benchmark and reliability summary

| Company | Retrieval [Recall\@5](mailto:Recall@5){.email} | SDG F1 | Stage Accuracy | Valid-item Precision | Over-claim Rate |
|------------|-----------:|-----------:|-----------:|-----------:|-----------:|
| Coromandel | 0.78 | 0.81 | 0.72 | 0.88 | 0.12 |
| JSW Steel | 0.76 | 0.79 | 0.74 | 0.86 | 0.14 |
| BASF India | 0.74 | 0.77 | 0.71 | 0.89 | 0.10 |
| Ambuja Cement | 0.77 | 0.80 | 0.75 | 0.87 | 0.11 |
| Aarti Industries Limited | 0.73 | 0.76 | 0.70 | 0.85 | 0.13 |
| GNFC | 0.75 | 0.78 | 0.73 | 0.86 | 0.12 |

**Sample interpretation:**\
Across the six Indian companies, retrieval [recall\@5](mailto:recall@5){.email} ranged from 0.73 to 0.78, while SDG-label F1 ranged from 0.76 to 0.81. Valid-item precision remained consistently high, between 0.85 and 0.89, suggesting that the validation layer removed a substantial share of unsupported claims. Over-claim rates remained below 0.15 for all firms, with the lowest rate observed for BASF India and the highest for JSW Steel.

# Table 2. Company-level substantive disclosure profile

| Company | Valid Evidence Units | Rhetoric Share | Commitment Share | Implementation Share | Measured Implementation Share | Mean Evidence Score | Dominant SDGs |
|---------|--------:|--------:|--------:|--------:|--------:|--------:|---------|
| Coromandel | 35 | 0.26 | 0.28 | 0.46 | 0.26 | 3.9 | SDG 2, SDG 12, SDG 13 |
| JSW Steel | 41 | 0.22 | 0.25 | 0.53 | 0.31 | 4.2 | SDG 8, SDG 9, SDG 12, SDG 13 |
| BASF India | 33 | 0.29 | 0.30 | 0.41 | 0.21 | 3.7 | SDG 6, SDG 12, SDG 13 |
| Ambuja Cement | 38 | 0.24 | 0.27 | 0.49 | 0.29 | 4.0 | SDG 9, SDG 11, SDG 12, SDG 13 |
| Aarti Industries Limited | 31 | 0.31 | 0.29 | 0.40 | 0.19 | 3.6 | SDG 6, SDG 8, SDG 12 |
| GNFC | 34 | 0.27 | 0.29 | 0.44 | 0.24 | 3.8 | SDG 2, SDG 6, SDG 12, SDG 13 |

**Sample interpretation:**\
JSW Steel and Ambuja Cement showed the highest implementation shares, suggesting more operationally embedded disclosure patterns. Coromandel and GNFC showed strong representation of SDG 2 alongside SDGs 12 and 13, consistent with their agribusiness and fertilizer-linked reporting context. BASF India and Aarti Industries Limited showed somewhat lower measured implementation shares, with disclosure more concentrated in responsible production, water, and process-related sustainability themes.

# Table 3. Longitudinal disclosure trend summary

| Company | Years Covered | Implementation Trend Slope | Rhetoric Trend Slope | Most Persistent SDGs | Interpretation |
|------------|-----------:|-----------:|-----------:|------------|------------|
| Coromandel | 2019–2025 | +0.05/year | -0.04/year | SDG 2, SDG 12, SDG 13 | Progressive shift from commitment language to implementation evidence |
| JSW Steel | 2019–2025 | +0.06/year | -0.03/year | SDG 8, SDG 9, SDG 12, SDG 13 | Strongest improvement in operational and measurable disclosure |
| BASF India | 2019–2025 | +0.03/year | -0.02/year | SDG 6, SDG 12, SDG 13 | Gradual improvement, but more moderate than heavy industry peers |
| Ambuja Cement | 2019–2025 | +0.05/year | -0.03/year | SDG 9, SDG 11, SDG 12, SDG 13 | Increasing emphasis on implementation and quantitative evidence |
| Aarti Industries Limited | 2019–2025 | +0.03/year | -0.01/year | SDG 6, SDG 8, SDG 12 | Incremental improvement with persistent policy-heavy language |
| GNFC | 2019–2025 | +0.04/year | -0.03/year | SDG 2, SDG 6, SDG 12, SDG 13 | Moderate transition toward more measurable implementation disclosure |

**Sample interpretation:**\
All six firms showed positive implementation-trend slopes and declining rhetoric-trend slopes, suggesting increasing disclosure maturity over time. The steepest implementation trend was observed for JSW Steel, while BASF India and Aarti Industries Limited showed more gradual movement.

# Table 4. Cross-model robustness summary

| Company | SDG Agreement (Model A vs Model B) | Stage Agreement | Difference in Over-claim Rate | Robustness Interpretation |
|---------------|--------------:|--------------:|--------------:|---------------|
| Coromandel | 0.82 | 0.76 | 0.04 | Good agreement, moderate stage sensitivity |
| JSW Steel | 0.79 | 0.74 | 0.06 | Strong content signal, slightly higher model variance |
| BASF India | 0.84 | 0.78 | 0.03 | Most stable across models |
| Ambuja Cement | 0.81 | 0.77 | 0.04 | Good cross-model consistency |
| Aarti Industries Limited | 0.77 | 0.72 | 0.05 | Moderate model sensitivity |
| GNFC | 0.80 | 0.75 | 0.04 | Reasonably stable across model choices |

**Sample interpretation:**\
Cross-model agreement remained acceptable across firms, with BASF India showing the strongest robustness and Aarti Industries Limited showing the greatest sensitivity to model choice. Stage agreement was consistently lower than SDG agreement, confirming that implementation-stage classification is the more difficult and unstable task.

# Table 5. India portfolio-level summary

| Metric                                                 |          Value |
|--------------------------------------------------------|---------------:|
| Total companies                                        |              6 |
| Average retrieval [recall\@5](mailto:recall@5){.email} |           0.76 |
| Average SDG F1                                         |           0.79 |
| Average stage accuracy                                 |           0.73 |
| Average valid-item precision                           |           0.87 |
| Average over-claim rate                                |           0.12 |
| Mean rhetoric share                                    |           0.27 |
| Mean commitment share                                  |           0.28 |
| Mean implementation share                              |           0.46 |
| Mean measured implementation share                     |           0.25 |
| Most frequently dominant SDGs                          | SDG 12, SDG 13 |
| Sector-linked SDG differentiation                      |        Present |

**Sample interpretation:**\
At the portfolio level, the six-company sample showed moderate-to-strong implementation-oriented SDG disclosure, with an average implementation share of 0.46 and a measured implementation share of 0.25. SDGs 12 and 13 appeared most consistently across sectors, while sector-linked specialization was visible in SDG 2 for agribusiness-linked firms and SDGs 9 and 11 for infrastructure- and materials-linked firms.

# Sample results paragraph for a paper

Across the six Indian companies, the proposed workflow demonstrated consistent benchmark performance, with retrieval [recall\@5](mailto:recall@5){.email} ranging from 0.73 to 0.78 and SDG-label F1 ranging from 0.76 to 0.81. Valid-item precision remained high across firms, indicating that deterministic validation substantially reduced unsupported extractions. Substantively, implementation-oriented disclosure exceeded rhetoric-oriented disclosure for all six companies, although the magnitude of this shift varied by sector. JSW Steel and Ambuja Cement exhibited the highest implementation shares and measured implementation shares, while Coromandel and GNFC showed stronger SDG 2 representation alongside SDGs 12 and 13. Longitudinally, all firms showed declining rhetoric shares and rising implementation shares, suggesting a broad transition toward more operational and measurable sustainability reporting over time.

# Important note

These values are **illustrative sample numbers**. They are suitable for: - structuring the paper - showing the format - drafting the results section

They are **not real results** until you run the pipeline on each company’s reports.
